steps.title: Identify the exact claim the quotation must support
steps.content: The passage says Richard is “not entirely ready to show subservience” to Bolingbroke. The right quotation must demonstrate that unreadiness concretely — ideally through language about not having learned or mastered the submissive behaviors a subject owes a king.

steps.title: Eliminate three choices, confirm one
steps.content:
• Choice A — '“Am I both priest and clerk? Well then, amen. / God save the King! although I be not he; / And yet, amen, if heaven do think him me.”': Richard questions Bolingbroke's legitimacy but says nothing about his own unreadiness to behave as a subject. ✗
  • Choice B — '“I have no name, no title,— / No, not that name was given me at the font,— / But ‘tis usurp’d:— Alack the heavy day, / That I have worn so many winters out, / And know not now what name to call myself!”': Focuses on Richard's lost identity, not on his unpreparedness for submission. ✗
  • Choice C — '“Still my griefs are mine. / You may my glories and my state depose, / But not my griefs; still am I king of those.”': Expresses emotional defiance but not an inability to perform subservient acts. ✗
  • Choice D — '“Alack, why am I sent for to a king, / Before I have shook off the regal thoughts / Wherewith I reign’d? I hardly yet have learn’d / To insinuate, flatter, bow, and bend my knee.”': “I hardly yet have learn’d / To insinuate, flatter, bow, and bend my knee” maps directly onto “not entirely ready to show subservience” — he hasn't learned the very behaviors subservience requires. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
